relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Samson sees a woman across a people-boundary and fixes his desire upon her.
He asks his parents to obtain her, while her own voice remains outside the record.
Family boundary language hardens around his choice, and Samson answers only that she is right in his eyes.
The story claims a hidden occasion within a compromised human situation; providence does not cleanse the harm people choose.
A lion roars in the vineyard as Samson descends.
Power rushes through him, and life is torn apart by bare hands.
He speaks with the woman, and desire remains his measure.
Later he turns toward the carcass and finds honey inside death.
He eats the sweetness in secrecy and gives it to his parents without telling its source.
A wedding feast gathers the social world around the marriage.
Thirty companions are placed beside him.
Samson turns feast into a high-stakes riddle game.
The wager binds clothing, pride, and group honor together.
Food from eater and sweetness from strength conceal a private experience.
The companions threaten the bride and her household with fire to escape their loss.
Her tears carry both relational strain and an unspoken terror the reader has heard.
Under sustained pressure, Samson gives the answer, and she gives it to those threatening her.
The men solve the riddle, and Samson answers by reducing his wife to a plowed animal.
He kills strangers to pay the wager, and anger carries him away from the marriage.
The woman is assigned to another man, her life rearranged again without her voice.

contemplative_note: Judges 14 is a web of unspoken consent, coercion, secrecy, and retaliatory honor. The honey inside the lion is not permission to romanticize violence; it is sweetness found inside a dead thing, surrounded by concealment. The chapter asks whether power can recognize another person's personhood before desire, pride, and group conflict consume them.
